items.passage: Hibernation is a survival strategy that lets certain animals endure long winters when food becomes scarce. During this state, an animal's body temperature drops sharply and its heart rate slows to just a few beats each minute. Ground squirrels, for example, can lower their internal temperature to nearly freezing for weeks at a time. Stored body fat supplies the small amount of energy these sleepers still require. When warmer weather finally returns, the animals gradually wake and resume their normal activity.

Sustainable agriculture aims to produce food while protecting the soil, water, and wildlife that farming depends on. Instead of relying heavily on chemical fertilizers, many farmers now rotate their crops to keep the soil naturally fertile. Planting cover crops between harvests prevents erosion and returns valuable nutrients to the ground. Some growers also welcome insects and birds that feed on common pests. These methods can lower costs over time and help farmland stay productive for future generations.

Spaced practice is one of the most effective ways for people to learn new material. Rather than studying for hours in a single session, learners review information in shorter blocks spread across several days. Each time the brain recalls a fact after a short delay, the memory grows stronger and more lasting. Researchers find that students who space their practice remember far more during later tests. Cramming may feel productive, but its benefits usually fade within a day or two.

The Silk Road was a vast network of trade routes that linked China with the markets of the Mediterranean. Merchants carried silk, spices, and precious stones across wide deserts and steep mountains on long, dangerous journeys. Few traders traveled the entire distance themselves, passing their goods instead from one region to the next. Ideas, religions, and inventions moved along these same routes alongside the cargo. In this way, distant civilizations gradually shaped one another for many centuries.

Earthquakes occur when stress built up inside the Earth's crust is suddenly released along a fault. The rocks on either side of the fault lock together for years until the strain finally overcomes the friction holding them. When they slip, energy radiates outward as waves that shake the ground violently. Scientists measure these waves with sensitive instruments known as seismographs. By studying the patterns carefully, researchers can estimate where future earthquakes are most likely to strike.

Mars has fascinated scientists because it may once have supported conditions suitable for life. Robotic rovers now crawl slowly across its dusty surface, drilling into rocks and analyzing their chemistry. These machines have discovered dried riverbeds and minerals that form only in the presence of water. Such evidence suggests that the planet was warmer and far wetter billions of years ago. Future missions may eventually return samples to Earth for much more detailed study.
